verses.commentary: Six days after Peter's confession. Three disciples — Peter, James, John (the inner circle). High mountain — Sinai echo. The chosen three witness what the others cannot yet bear.
Transfigured (metemorphothe, μετεμορφώθη) before them. Face shone like the sun (Moses-language, Exodus 34:29). Clothes white as light. The hidden glory becomes visible.
Moses and Elijah appear, talking with Jesus. Law (Moses) and Prophets (Elijah) — both had mountain encounters with God (Sinai, Horeb). Now they confer with the one to whom they testified.
Peter offers to build three booths — Feast of Tabernacles imagery (Sukkot, Leviticus 23). He wants to extend the vision. Three booths levels Jesus with Moses and Elijah — a categorical error.

Bright cloud (echo of Sinai cloud, Exodus 24:15-18). Father's voice repeats the baptism declaration (3:17): 'This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased.' Added: 'Listen to him' (akouete autou, ἀκούετε αὐτοῦ) — Deuteronomy 18:15, the prophet like Moses.
Disciples fall on faces, terrified. Theophany response is consistent across scripture — humans cannot remain standing in unmediated divine presence.
Jesus touches them: 'Rise, do not be afraid.' Tenderness in the wake of terror. The transcendent Christ retains his human accessibility.
Look up — only Jesus. Moses and Elijah withdraw. Christology's punchline: when the Law and Prophets fade, Jesus alone remains. He fulfills, then absorbs, their testimony.
Coming down: tell no one until the Son of Man is raised. The transfiguration must be read through resurrection. Premature disclosure would distort the meaning.
Disciples' question: scribes say Elijah must come first (Malachi 4:5). How does this fit with what we just saw?
Yes — Elijah comes and restores all things. Jesus affirms the prophecy.
But Elijah has already come — they did not recognize him, did whatever they wished. Likewise the Son of Man will suffer. John = Elijah typologically; his fate foreshadows Jesus'.
Disciples understand: he spoke of John the Baptist. The forerunner's beheading was the script's preview.
Down to the crowd. A man kneels — desperate father.
'Lord, have mercy on my son.' He suffers seizures (seleniazetai, σεληνιάζεται — moonstruck), falls into fire and water. Real medical description, possibly epileptic, demonically intensified.
Disciples could not heal. The Twelve, who received authority over demons (10:1), fail. The contrast with the transfigured Christ is sharp.

'O faithless and twisted generation, how long must I be with you? How long must I bear with you?' Frustration with persistent unbelief — including the disciples' lapse. Even Jesus' patience has dimension.
Jesus rebukes (epetimesen, ἐπετίμησεν) the demon. Healing is exorcism here. Immediate cure.
Disciples privately: 'Why could we not cast it out?' The honest post-mortem of failed ministry.

'Because of your little faith.' Mustard seed faith — enough to move mountains. The smallest genuine faith outperforms the most ambitious faithless effort. Mountain-moving is hyperbole for impossibility-attempting.

[Textual variant absent from the earliest manuscripts. Later manuscripts add: 'But this kind does not go out except by prayer and fasting.' Many modern translations omit it as a scribal harmonization with Mark 9:29.]

Second passion prediction (compare 16:21; 20:18-19). 'The Son of Man is about to be delivered (paradidosthai, παραδίδοσθαι, handed over) into the hands of men.' The passive verb hints at divine plan beneath human agency.
They will kill him; on the third day he will be raised. Disciples are 'deeply grieved.' Death registered; resurrection clause not yet processed.
Capernaum. Two-drachma temple tax collectors approach Peter. 'Does your teacher pay the temple tax?' (Exodus 30:13 — half-shekel).
Peter answers yes — then Jesus pre-empts the conversation indoors: 'From whom do earthly kings take taxes? From their sons or from others?' The Socratic setup.
From others. 'Then the sons are free.' Jesus implies: the temple is his Father's house; he is exempt as the Son.

But to avoid offense (skandalizo, σκανδαλίζω) — go fishing, find a coin in the first fish, pay for us both. The accommodation is generous: rights surrendered for peace. The miracle is incidental to the lesson on yielded rights.
